metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC4's `git diff --name-only` file list to include the `IdentityConvention` source file (e.g. `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` or equivalent) so a correct implementation isn't rejected by the criterion.
evidence: AC4: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and files under `tests/`'
Approach: 'S2 introduces `IdentityConvention` as a new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`'
The IdentityConvention object is described as distinct from the JiraBackend thin-wrapper in `adapters/jira/backend.py`
findings.finding: AC4's `git diff --name-only` whitelist names only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and `tests/` — but the Approach section explicitly introduces `IdentityConvention` as a 'new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`' that is distinct from `backend.py`. A correct implementation would produce an additional file (e.g. `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py`) that is not on AC4's list, causing the AC to fail on a valid implementation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; Approach section — IdentityConvention paragraph
scenarios: An executor faithfully implements IdentityConvention in its own file under adapters/jira/ (the natural layout) and passes all functional tests, but AC4's git-diff check shows an unexpected file and the criterion reports failure.
Alternatively, an executor stuffs IdentityConvention into backend.py to satisfy AC4, conflating two distinct responsibilities and obscuring the clean separation the plan calls for.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC4 to: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` (or the chosen name for the IdentityConvention object), and files under `tests/`.'

findings.finding: The plan attributes `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `OutboundMapper` ('OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'), but a full read of `_map_local_to_jira_fields` (outbound_fields.py:112-198) and a search of outbound_fields.py for 'fit_text_to_adf' confirm the function is never called there. `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is called from `_diff_fields` (outbound_fields.py:567) — the field-comparison path, not the mapping path. Attributing it to OutboundMapper is a false premise: the Protocol's method surface derived from the de-facto call sites would not include `fit_text_to_adf_limit` under the mapping role.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `(+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)` from the `OutboundMapper` derivation in the Approach section. If ADF truncation must be part of the backend interface, assign it to the role Protocol whose de-facto surface includes `_diff_fields` (likely a combined OutboundMapper that wraps both map + diff, or a separate FieldNormalizer role), and update the contract test assertion accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan places `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` under `OutboundMapper` (`OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)`), but the actual codebase shows `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is NOT called inside `_map_local_to_jira_fields` — it is called from `_diff_fields` (the differ/comparison path at `outbound_fields.py:567`) and from `inbound_differ.py:192`. The plan's own stated derivation principle is to 'derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls', which would place `fit_text_to_adf_limit` in `FieldSanitizer` (already backed by `_sanitize_description`, which itself calls `fit_text_to_adf_limit`), not in `OutboundMapper`. Misattributing it to `OutboundMapper` would produce a Protocol that doesn't match actual call-site boundaries, complicating the S4 rewiring where sites call `fit_text_to_adf_limit` via the FieldSanitizer path (description send-side truncation) rather than the mapper path.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: `OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields` (remove `+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit`); add `fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `FieldSanitizer` alongside `_sanitize_description` (which already delegates to it), consistent with `adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:18` importing it for that purpose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `IdentityConvention`'s exact home file in the Edit-set (either confirm it lives inside `adapters/jira/backend.py`, or enumerate it as a third new file such as `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py`) and update AC #4's git-diff clause to name it.
evidence: Edit-set names only: 'New src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py' and 'New src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py' and new tests.

Approach says: 'S2 introduces IdentityConvention as a **new, self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/**' — 'under adapters/jira/' leaves open whether it is inside backend.py or a separate file.

AC #4: 'verified by git diff --name-only touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/' — this check would fail silently if IdentityConvention lands in a third adapter file.
No third file is enumerated anywhere in the plan for IdentityConvention.
findings.finding: The `IdentityConvention` object is described in Approach as 'a new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`' but the plan's Edit-set only enumerates two new files: `_backend.py` and `adapters/jira/backend.py`. If `IdentityConvention` is co-located inside `adapters/jira/backend.py` this is fine, but the plan never pins that co-location — it might land in its own third file (e.g., `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py`). AC #4's git-diff verification clause names only `_backend.py` and `adapters/jira/backend.py`, which would falsify if a third file is created.
scenarios: Implementer places IdentityConvention in adapters/jira/identity_convention.py or adapters/jira/convention.py; AC #4 git-diff check falsely passes (the clause doesn't mention that file) while the edit-set is incomplete.
A reviewer running AC #4 mechanically sees only the two named files and misses the new third file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Edit-set and Approach: 'IdentityConvention is defined inside `adapters/jira/backend.py` (co-located with JiraBackend)' — or if it warrants its own file, add `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` to the Edit-set and the AC #4 git-diff check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `add_label` (and any other `AcliGraphMixin` methods used at identity-write sites) to the `TicketTransport` Protocol surface enumeration in the Approach section, confirming the de-facto call surface is complete before implementation.
evidence: Plan text: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient'
Grep confirms client.add_label at dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, apply_inbound_records.py:223 — all three are identity-write sites the plan explicitly names
acli_graph.py:77: `def add_label(self, jira_key: str, label: str) -> None` — on AcliGraphMixin, not named in the TicketTransport method list
IdentityConvention per plan only formats/parses strings; it does not issue the transport call — so add_label must be on TicketTransport
findings.finding: The plan asserts `TicketTransport` is derived from 'the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`', but `add_label` — used at all three identity-write sites the plan itself cites (dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, apply_inbound_records.py:223) — is not listed in the `TicketTransport` method enumeration. `add_label` lives on `AcliGraphMixin` (acli_graph.py:77), which `AcliClient` inherits, but if it is absent from the Protocol, `JiraBackend`'s `IdentityConvention` object cannot issue the label write via the transport, and the contract suite cannot verify delegation of that call.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`'
scenarios: Implementer derives TicketTransport only from the five listed verbs (create/read/update/transition/comment) and omits add_label; JiraBackend then cannot satisfy IdentityConvention's write path through the transport, requiring a design change in S4.
Contract suite cannot assert that a backend's TicketTransport correctly delegates add_label calls, leaving a delegation regression undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach bullet for TicketTransport, extend the method list: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment **and `add_label`** methods on `acli.AcliClient` / `AcliGraphMixin`'. Also add `set_entity_property` (used alongside `add_label` at all three identity-write sites) to the enumeration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the IdentityConvention spec to explicitly cover the dual-form convention: the object should either (a) stamp colon-form and parse BOTH 'rebar-id:' and 'rebar-id-' prefixes (documenting the legacy form), or (b) explicitly state that legacy-hyphen recognition is out-of-scope for this object and remains inline — so the 'single source of the format' claim is scoped accurately and S4 implementers are not surprised by the gap.
evidence: Plan states IdentityConvention 'formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef (the single source of the format those four sites currently hand-inline).'
binding_walk.py:341: `for prefix in ('rebar-id:', 'rebar-id-'):` — dual-form detection, not listed as a plan site
binding_store.py:526: `hyphen_label = f'rebar-id-{local_id}'` — legacy hyphen write for recovery, not listed
outbound_differ.py:302: `_EXCLUDED_PREFIXES: tuple[str, ...] = ('rebar-id:', 'rebar-id-', ...)` — exclusion list using both forms
rebar_id_audit.py:178: `return target == 'label' and payload.startswith('rebar-id-')` — hyphen-form prefix check
ABSENCE: the plan does not acknowledge the legacy hyphen-form as part of the convention or as an out-of-scope gap
findings.finding: The IdentityConvention object as specified covers only the canonical colon-form (rebar-id:{remote_id} stamp and parse), but the live identity convention the codebase actually enforces is dual-form: colon ('rebar-id:') for canonical writes and legacy hyphen ('rebar-id-') for backwards-compatible reads/recovery. The dual-form is active in at least 8 core sites not listed in the plan (differ.py:91-92, outbound_differ.py:302, inbound_differ.py:251, inbound_translate.py:76-77, rebar_id_audit.py multiple sites, reconcile_check.py:389/403, apply_inbound.py:324, binding_store.py:526), all of which scan for BOTH prefixes. The IdentityConvention contract test as described ('stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip') tests only the colon path. If S4 routes callers through this object as a complete single source of truth, callers expecting a parse-both-forms interface will be surprised — or the object will silently be incomplete and the legacy-form logic stays inline everywhere else.
findings.location: Approach section — IdentityConvention definition; Testing section — 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'
scenarios: S4 routes binding_walk.py through IdentityConvention.parse(), which only handles 'rebar-id:' — legacy hyphen-form issues are no longer detected as bound, breaking the L10 guard for pre-migration Jira issues.
The contract test round-trips only the colon form and passes green even though the convention object is incomplete, giving false confidence that the convention is fully captured.
findings.suggested_fix: In the IdentityConvention spec under Approach, add a sentence: 'The convention object exposes two methods: stamp(ref: RemoteRef) → str (colon-form only) and parse(label: str) → RemoteRef | None (recognises both colon and legacy hyphen forms, returning None for non-matching strings). The legacy hyphen-form (rebar-id-{local_id}) is read-only recognised for backwards compatibility with pre-migration Jira issues; new writes always use the colon form.' Update the contract test description to include a parse(hyphen-form-label) → RemoteRef assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify IdentityConvention's method names and signatures (e.g. label_for(ref: RemoteRef) -> str and parse_label(label: str) -> RemoteRef | None), and explicitly state whether parse_label must also accept the legacy 'rebar-id-' hyphen prefix (the form still active at binding_walk.py:341).
evidence: Plan says IdentityConvention 'formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef' — no method names given
binding_walk.py:341 iterates 'for prefix in ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-")' — the hyphen legacy form is an active code path
Contract test spec says 'stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip' — only the colon form is implied; hyphen parsing is unmentioned

The plan explicitly notes 'the four core call sites are NOT rewired to it here' meaning the legacy-label scan at binding_walk.py:341 is not covered in S2 scope — but an S2 IdentityConvention that cannot parse hyphen-form labels would break S4's rewire
findings.finding: IdentityConvention is described as a 'new, self-contained pure convention object' with 'no single existing function to delegate to', but the plan never specifies its method names or signatures. The Testing section says the contract test exercises 'stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip', implying at least two methods (format/stamp and parse), but neither is named. Furthermore, binding_walk.py:341 shows the codebase currently tolerates BOTH 'rebar-id:' (colon) and 'rebar-id-' (hyphen) prefix forms in parse/scan operations, yet the plan only specifies the colon format. Whether IdentityConvention.parse must also handle the hyphen legacy form to be contract-correct is unresolved.
findings.location: Approach section — 'IdentityConvention' bullet; Testing section — 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'
scenarios: Executor names the methods format() and parse() while a different executor names them stamp() and find(), making S4 consumers incompatible with the S2 interface

IdentityConvention.parse() only handles 'rebar-id:{id}' colon form; when S4 routes binding_walk.py:341 through it, hyphen-labelled legacy tickets are no longer recognized as bound, causing duplicate-create errors
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'IdentityConvention protocol: label_for(ref: RemoteRef) -> str — returns "rebar-id:{ref.remote_id}"; parse_label(label: str) -> RemoteRef | None — matches both "rebar-id:" (canonical) and "rebar-id-" (legacy tolerance, mirroring binding_walk.py:341) prefixes and returns a RemoteRef on match or None on no-match. Round-trip contract: parse_label(label_for(ref)) == ref.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient`, but `transition_issue` is a module-level function in `acli.py` that instantiates its own AcliClient using `resolve_jira_settings()` — it is NOT an AcliClient instance method. The actual AcliClient method is `transition_issue_by_name(jira_key, target_status)`. Because the plan says Protocol signatures are 'derived from the de-facto surface the core already calls', the TicketTransport Protocol's transition method signature and name are under-specified: the implementor cannot determine whether to model the module-level function signature or the instance method signature.
findings.location: Approach section: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace 'transition methods on acli.AcliClient' with 'AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name(jira_key, target_status) — the instance method (not the module-level acli.transition_issue, which self-constructs a client from env)' to pin the correct delegation target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve where IdentityConvention lives (its own file under adapters/jira/ OR inside adapters/jira/backend.py) and update AC4's git-diff file list to explicitly include that file so the verification is accurate.
evidence: Approach: 'S2 introduces IdentityConvention as a new, self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/ that formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef'
AC4: 'verified by git diff --name-only touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/'
Edit-set in Context names exactly two new Python files: _backend.py and adapters/jira/backend.py — IdentityConvention's file home is not listed
findings.finding: The Approach section describes IdentityConvention as 'a new, self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/' — implying a new file (e.g. adapters/jira/identity_convention.py) — but AC4's git diff guard lists only '_backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/'. If IdentityConvention lives in its own file, that file is silently excluded from the AC4 verification, making the check incomplete. The Edit-set (Context) also lists only two new Python files, leaving the physical home of IdentityConvention unspecified.
findings.location: Approach section ('S2 introduces IdentityConvention as a new, self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/') vs. AC4 ('git diff --name-only touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/')
scenarios: Implementer creates adapters/jira/identity_convention.py; the AC4 git-diff check fails/is wrong because it doesn't list that file
Implementer stuffs IdentityConvention into backend.py to satisfy AC4, conflicting with the 'self-contained' intent in Approach
findings.suggested_fix: If IdentityConvention gets its own file (e.g. adapters/jira/identity_convention.py), add it to the Edit-set list and update AC4 to read: 'touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, adapters/jira/identity_convention.py, and files under tests/'. If it lives inside backend.py, add a sentence in Approach clarifying that co-location is intentional.

findings.finding: AC4's `git diff --name-only` proving command lacks an explicit baseline reference, leaving the executor to guess what to diff against (HEAD~1? pre-story main? a named tag?). Without a baseline, the command can silently pass even if core files were touched in an earlier commit of the same branch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'No core differ/apply module is modified by this story — verified by `git diff --name-only`...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: 'verified by `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD | grep -v -E "^(src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend\.py|src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend\.py|tests/)"` producing no output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) and their isinstance-gated dispatch are needed now vs. deferrable to the story that first wires a production consumer — or explicitly note in the plan why introducing the gating in S2 (before any production code uses it) is preferable to landing it alongside S4's rewire or the first real second backend (epic be74).
evidence: grep for 'isinstance(backend' in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler returns zero hits — no production isinstance-capability dispatch exists today
grep for 'SupportsLinks' returns zero hits in the codebase
Existing @runtime_checkable uses: outbound_differ.py:228-233 (BindingStoreProtocol for type annotation only), inbound_differ.py:78-82 (same) — neither uses isinstance for capability gating

Plan states: 'the three capability Protocols are declared @runtime_checkable, which is already the reconciler's established pattern (outbound_differ.py:228, inbound_differ.py)' — but those uses are structural subtyping for function parameters, not capability dispatch
No S4 ticket (6673-7636-a116-4f90) references isinstance(backend, Supports*) gates — S4 rewires to Backend methods directly
The second backend (which would diverge on capability dispatch) is deferred to epic be74, outside this epic's scope
findings.finding: The three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) and their isinstance-gated dispatch machinery are introduced in S2 with zero production consumers — the dispatch is exercised only through FakeBackend in the contract test suite. No real second backend exists (deferred to epic be74), and no production code routes through isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks) dispatch in S2, S3, or S4 (S4 routes call sites through Backend methods, not through capability gates). The plan's justification — 'the reconciler's established pattern' — overstates: the existing @runtime_checkable Protocols in outbound_differ.py:229 and inbound_differ.py:79 are used only as type-annotation guards at function signatures (BindingStoreProtocol), not for capability-gated isinstance dispatch. The capability-dispatch pattern is net-new.
findings.location: Context / Problem: '_backend.py' — 'three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental)'; Testing: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links)'
scenarios: The capability-dispatch machinery is spec'd and tested in S2 but may diverge from how S4 actually wires call sites (S4 could end up calling Backend methods unconditionally), leaving the SupportsLinks/SupportsComments/SupportsIncremental gating as dead design surface.

If a future second backend does NOT implement SupportsLinks and the dispatch gate is real, this is justified; but if S4 routes all consumers directly through Backend role methods without isinstance gates, the capability Protocols' dispatch semantics are never invoked in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the `RemoteRef` field types (`str`) and mutability (`@dataclass(frozen=True)`) explicitly in the plan so the executor does not need to guess and the identity type is fully pinned as ADR 0035 intends.
evidence: Plan: 'RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} dataclass' — no types or mutability stated
AC1: 'RemoteRef(vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…) constructs' — does not assert frozenness or field types
The plan calls RemoteRef a 'pinned' type in ADR 0035 §(d), implying it is a stable contract worth specifying precisely
findings.finding: The `RemoteRef` dataclass's field types and mutability contract are not stated, leaving an executor to guess whether fields are `str`, whether the dataclass is `frozen=True` (required for hashability when used as a dict key or set member), and whether any field has a default. This is a defaultable gap but should be stated given RemoteRef is described as a 'pinned' identity type in ADR 0035.
findings.location: Context/Problem — RemoteRef mention; Approach — IdentityConvention paragraph; AC1
scenarios: Executor creates a mutable dataclass; a later story uses RemoteRef as a dict key and gets a TypeError because unhashable mutable dataclass.
Executor uses non-string types (e.g. int for remote_id) that don't round-trip through the label format tested by IdentityConvention.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `_backend.py` description: '`RemoteRef` is `@dataclass(frozen=True)` with fields `vendor: str`, `instance: str`, `remote_id: str` — all required, no defaults.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add IdentityConvention to the AC1 import-assertion list (or add a dedicated AC line) so that its existence and basic construction are verified at the same tier as Backend and RemoteRef.
evidence: AC1: 'importable via from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._backend import Backend, RemoteRef … verified by an import test that asserts each name resolves'
Approach: IdentityConvention is a substantive new deliverable with its own format/parse contract — 'stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip'
No AC names IdentityConvention in its subject or verify step
findings.finding: IdentityConvention is a described deliverable (Approach section, several paragraphs) but has no Acceptance Criterion that verifies it is importable and correctly constructed — unlike Backend and RemoteRef which get an explicit import+construction test in AC1. The contract suite exercises its round-trip behavior, but no AC names 'IdentityConvention' in its verify step.
findings.location: Approach section (IdentityConvention description) vs. AC1 (covers only Backend and RemoteRef imports)
scenarios: IdentityConvention is accidentally omitted or misnamed in the implementation; no AC import check catches it — only the round-trip contract test (AC2) would notice if the class fails to construct
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 to include: 'and IdentityConvention (from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.identity_convention or adapters/jira/backend) constructs and round-trips a RemoteRef label' — or add a sub-bullet to AC2 naming IdentityConvention explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update ADR 0035 (or add a dedicated ADR/design-doc section) to record the S2 decisions: the `Backend` facade shape, capability-detection via `isinstance` on `@runtime_checkable` Protocols (and the explicit rejection of a `capabilities()` method), `RemoteRef` struct fields, and the deferred-to-S4 scope boundary of `IdentityConvention`.
evidence: 'the `RemoteRef` identity type pinned in ADR 0035 §(d) [rebar:ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb]'
'Capability-detection mechanism (pinned here): callers … detect an opt-in capability with `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)` … There is no `capabilities()` method.'
No mention of updating ADR 0035 or creating a new ADR/design-doc to record S2 decisions.
T5e criterion: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions (capability-detection via `isinstance` on `@runtime_checkable` Protocols with no `capabilities()` method; the shape and responsibilities of the `Backend` facade and its five role Protocols; the `RemoteRef` struct; the scope boundary for `IdentityConvention`) but does not specify that ADR 0035 will be updated — or a new ADR/design-doc section created — to capture these decisions. ADR 0035 is referenced only as prior motivation (§(a)/§(b) inventory), not as a living document that will record the S2 resolutions.
findings.location: Context / Problem (ADR 0035 references), Approach (capability-detection mechanism pinned), Scope section
scenarios: A future developer adding a third backend needs to know the authoritative capability-detection contract; without a doc update they must reconstruct it from source.
If S4 or S5 revisit the `IdentityConvention` scope boundary, the rationale for deferral is only present in this story ticket, not in a persistent ADR.
A reviewer auditing why `capabilities()` was rejected will find no recorded rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan bullet under Scope: 'Update ADR 0035 §(d) (or append a new §(e)) to record: (1) Backend facade Protocol breakdown; (2) capability detection = `isinstance` on `@runtime_checkable` Protocols, no `capabilities()` method; (3) `RemoteRef` fields; (4) `IdentityConvention` is a new pure object introduced in S2 but not wired into core until S4.' This requires no new file — just a section in the existing ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a method-level vocabulary table to the Approach section (or a dedicated 'Protocol Signatures' subsection) enumerating each Protocol's method names, parameter types, and return types — at minimum for TicketTransport (list which AcliClient methods are included) and OutboundMapper (clarify whether _map_local_to_jira_fields and fit_text_to_adf_limit are one method or two, and what the Protocol-facing signature is).
evidence: Plan says 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient' — no method names listed

AcliClient has ~12 distinct public methods (search_issues, get_parent_map, get_comment_map, get_issuelinks_map, add_label, remove_label, transition_issue_by_name, update_priority, create_issue, update_issue, add_comment, get_issue) — plan provides no selection criterion

Plan says 'OutboundMapper ← _map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)' — _map_local_to_jira_fields has 4 parameters (ticket, binding_store, local_ticket_types, emit_detach_clear); whether these collapse into one Protocol method or two is unspecified
No _backend.py exists in repo (confirmed by search for 'TicketTransport', 'OutboundMapper', 'InboundMapper', 'FieldSanitizer', 'IdentityConvention', 'RemoteRef' — all returned no matches)
findings.finding: The five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) and three capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are named but their method signatures — names, parameter types, and return types — are never enumerated in the plan. The plan says only 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient' without listing which methods, and 'OutboundMapper ← _map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)' without specifying how those two functions compose into one Protocol method. AcliClient alone exposes ~12 methods (search_issues, get_parent_map, get_comment_map, get_issuelinks_map, add_label, remove_label, transition_issue_by_name, update_priority, create_issue, update_issue, add_comment, get_issue), with no indication of which are in scope for TicketTransport. An executor implementing _backend.py from this plan cannot determine Protocol method names or signatures without separately auditing the codebase.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient' and parallel bullets
scenarios: Implementer writes TicketTransport with only 'create/read/update/transition/comment' methods but misses add_label (used by IdentityConvention stamp sites), breaking the contract test that verifies stamp-and-find via transport

Implementer merges _map_local_to_jira_fields and fit_text_to_adf_limit into one OutboundMapper.map() call, but the contract suite expects two separate Protocol methods — or vice versa — causing a false RED or a tautological GREEN
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add a table for each Protocol: e.g. 'TicketTransport: create_issue(**fields) → dict, search_issues(jql, *, start_at, max_results) → list[dict], add_label(key, label) → None, remove_label(key, label) → None, transition_issue_by_name(key, name) → None, add_comment(key, body) → None, get_parent_map(...) → dict, get_comment_map(...) → dict, get_issuelinks_map(...) → dict'. For OutboundMapper: 'map_to_remote(ticket, *, binding_store, local_ticket_types, emit_detach_clear) → dict'. This table becomes the normative spec that both _backend.py and the JiraBackend delegation implement against.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
